title: Round Outdoor Adirondack Side Table
summary: A round 1x4-slat outdoor side table on collapsible X-leg supports, sized to match Adirondack chair height.
description:
The round top starts life as a rectangle: eight 1x4 boards of four different lengths, screwed to two underside supports with a 1/2" gap between each board, THEN traced into a circle from a pencil on a string and cut freehand with a jigsaw. Get the boards square and evenly gapped before you draw the circle — the circle-cutting step can't fix a crooked layout underneath it.

The X-shaped leg assembly is genuinely collapsible if you build it that way: swap the screws called out at the leg-support and center-attachment steps for removable bolts, and the table folds flat for winter storage. If you don't need that, screws are simpler and just as strong for a table that stays out year-round.

materials.name: Cedar, 1x4, 10 ft
materials.note: Weather-resistant species recommended; pallet wood also works.

materials.name: 1-1/2" to 2" self-tapping exterior screws
materials.note: Star bit — a standard bit strips out.

cutList.note: Underside cross-pieces holding the boards before the circle is cut.
Both ends cut at 45°, parallel to each other.

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: From the two 10 ft cedar 1x4s cut the eight tabletop boards in four matched pairs: two at 31 1/2", two at 30 7/16", two at 27 1/16" and two at 20". Those four lengths are the chords of a 31 1/2" circle, which is why they are odd numbers — cut them accurately and keep each pair together. From the three 1x2s cut two tabletop supports at 29 3/4", two leg supports at 23 1/2", two spacers at 3 1/2", four legs at 30" with both ends cut at 45 degrees and parallel to each other, and two base support stretchers at 17 1/2". Cut the four legs first with the saw set once so they come out identical; they are the longest and the only angled parts, and a leg even slightly off will make the X sit crooked. Both 1x4s and all three 1x2s are used nearly to the end, so there is no spare for a miscut.

steps.title: Build and cut the round tabletop
steps.body: Lay the eight boards out symmetrically about the center — the two 31 1/2" boards in the middle, then the 30 7/16" pair, the 27 1/16" pair, and the 20" pair on the outside — leaving a 1/2" gap between each. Eight 3 1/2" boards plus seven 1/2" gaps come to 31 1/2" across, the same as the longest board, so check that measurement before you fasten anything: it is the plan telling you the layout is right. Working outward from the center, screw the two 29 3/4" tabletop supports across the underside with 1-1/2" to 2" self-tapping exterior screws and glue. Then pin a string at the exact center of the assembly, tie a pencil at 15 3/4" — half of 31 1/2" — and swing the full circle, cut it freehand with the jigsaw staying just outside the line, and sand the edge fair. The circle cannot fix a crooked layout underneath it, so square everything up before the pencil comes out.

steps.title: Attach the leg supports
steps.body: Round the corners of the two 23 1/2" leg supports on the sander before they go on — they sit at shin height under an outdoor table and a square corner finds a leg. Lay them on the underside of the finished top and attach with 1-1/2" self-tapping exterior screws and glue — 2" would poke through the 1-1/2" of stacked 3/4" stock. Keep them parallel to each other and set the same distance either side of center: the X legs hang off these, so any offset here shows up as a lean in the finished table.

steps.title: Add the spacers
steps.body: Round the corners of the two 3 1/2" spacers and attach them to the leg supports with 1-1/2" self-tapping exterior screws and glue. Their job is to pack the support out by the thickness of a 1x2 so the two legs of each pair end up in different planes and can cross past one another — without them the legs would collide at the top. Keep the glue off the faces the legs will pivot against if you are building the collapsible version.

steps.title: Attach the legs
steps.body: Each leg is cut at 45 degrees at both ends with the two cuts parallel, so when the leg leans at 45 degrees the top cut sits flat against the leg support and the bottom cut sits flat on the ground — set them that way and the table stands level without any trimming. Fasten each leg at the top through the leg support and its spacer. Use a single fastener per top joint rather than two: a second screw locks the joint and kills the fold. For a collapsible table, put a removable bolt here instead of a screw.

steps.title: Connect the legs at center
steps.body: Stand the table up, let the legs settle into the spread you want, and fasten the crossing pair to each other at the point where they overlap — again with one fastener, which is the pivot the whole X turns on. Check the top is level and the legs are splayed the same amount on both sides before you drive it. For a collapsible table use a removable bolt here as well; these two joints and the leg tops are the only things holding the X, so they take the load either way.

steps.title: Add the base supports
steps.body: Attach the two 17 1/2" base support stretchers between the legs near the bottom, one per side, with the legs standing at their finished spread. Measure the spread at the top and set the bottom to match so the X stays symmetrical rather than leaning. These stretchers are what stop the base walking sideways when someone sets a drink down. Fasten with the exterior screws and skip the glue here — if the table is meant to collapse, this is the piece that comes off, so you want fasteners you can back out cleanly.

images.alt: A round cedar outdoor side table with collapsible X-shaped leg supports
